summary:
Captain James Cook's Three Voyages (1768–1779 AD) were the most significant voyages of exploration of the 18th century, completing the geographic knowledge of the Pacific Ocean, disproving the existence of the mythical southern continent Terra Australis (as imagined), mapping the coastlines of New Zealand, eastern Australia, the Pacific Northwest, the Hawaiian Islands, and dozens of Pacific island groups, and paving the way for British colonization of the Pacific. Cook, a self-educated Yorkshire man of extraordinary navigational skill and scientific mind, was selected by the Royal Society to command the Endeavour's first voyage (1768–1771), ostensibly to observe the transit of Venus from Tahiti — with secret orders to search for the southern continent.

Cook's three voyages transformed European geographic knowledge: the First Voyage mapped New Zealand and the eastern coast of Australia, claiming both for Britain; the Second Voyage (1772–1775) circumnavigated Antarctica without finding the continent (disproving Terra Australis as a temperate, habitable land) and surveyed the southern Pacific; the Third Voyage (1776–1779) mapped the Pacific Northwest of North America (seeking the Northwest Passage), discovered and named the Hawaiian Islands (the Sandwich Islands), and ended with Cook's death at the hands of Hawaiians in Kealakekua Bay in February 1779 — a confrontation whose exact causes remain debated. Cook's expeditions carried naturalists (Joseph Banks on the first voyage) whose botanical and zoological collections enormously enriched European natural history; Banks's Botany Bay became the name of the first British settlement site in Australia. The voyages' legacy includes both the scientific revolution in Pacific knowledge and the colonization that followed — of [[event:2464|Australia]] (1788) and eventually New Zealand.
